TailMux

# TailMux

Launching today

## Multiple Tailscale tailnets at once, no switching + no VM

4 followers

## Multiple Tailscale tailnets at once, no switching + no VM

4 followers

Visit website

VPN client

The official Tailscale client keeps one tailnet active at a time. TailMux makes work and personal tailnets reachable simultaneously on macOS and Linux, without switching accounts, running multiple system daemons, or using a VM. It runs an isolated embedded node per profile and routes by hostname, with strict no-fallback isolation. Use SSH, RDP/SMB, browsers, curl, git, and npm across tailnets at the same time.

 Overview
 Reviews
 Alternatives
 Team
 More

Payment Required

Launch tags:Mac•Developer Tools•Security

Launch Team

Subscribe

Blocks.ai [...] Hey Product Hunt 👋 I'm the maker. TailMux started as a personal annoyance: I run a work tailnet and a personal one, and the official client can only be active on one at a time. Fast user switching means constantly running tailscale switch and dropping the other connection. The workarounds (two daemons, userspace SOCKS5, a whole VM) all felt clunky on macOS, so I built something that runs an isolated embedded Tailscale node per tailnet and routes traffic by hostname suffix. Both tailnets are live at the same time, with strict isolation so nothing leaks between them. It covers SSH, RDP/SMB tunnels, per-tailnet browser routing, and routing curl/git/npm. It's a one-time $4.99 license (a year of updates, keep your version forever). It's not affiliated with Tailscale — just a companion tool. It [...] Launch Team

### What are you trying to do?

Log into multiple tailscale accounts at once, on the same device, to access all the machines on both networks simultaneously.

FR to switch between accounts quickly, and only be able to access machines on one tailnet at a time: #713

results.title: How to Connect to Two Tailscale Networks (Home and Work) on One ...
results.content:
• The second Tailscale (userspace + SOCKS5) is a proxy-based approach to your work environment.

• No IP Conflicts: Because each Tailscale runs in its own domain, you don’t have to worry if both home and work networks use overlapping IP ranges. The home Tailscale sets system routes for any subnets you accept. The work Tailscale only routes traffic that’s explicitly going through the SOCKS proxy.

• Start Up on Boot: For a long-term setup, consider creating a separate systemd service for the second Tailscale instance, or run it inside a tmux session.

• Security: If you open up the SOCKS5 port to the entire Tailscale interface, be sure you trust whomever can connect. By default, it listens on 127.0.0.1, so only local processes can use it.

Conclusion [...] You can run it in the foreground at first, to see logs. (Later, you might want to run it in a screen, tmux session, or systemd service.)

## Get Peter M’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.

Subscribe

Subscribe

- [x] 

Remember me for faster sign in

 

Part 3: Log In to Your Work Tailnet

Since this daemon is brand-new, it doesn’t know who you are yet. Let’s authenticate:

1.   In a new terminal, create a little wrapper script so the CLI knows which socket to talk to. For instance, call it ~/bin/tailscale-work:

#!/bin/bash

exec tailscale - socket="$HOME/.config/tailscale-work/foo.sock" "$@"

Make it executable:

chmod +x ~/bin/tailscale-work
(and ensure ~/bin is in your $PATH).

2. Log in: [...] -state="$HOME/.config/tailscale-work/foo.state" \

 -socks5-server=127.0.0.1:1080
   — tun=userspace-networking: Tailscale runs entirely in user space, no root privileges needed.
   — socket=…: so it doesn’t clash with /var/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock.
   — state=…: a separate file for storing keys and preferences.
   — socks5-server=127.0.0.1:1080: sets up a SOCKS5 proxy on port 1080. This port can be any free port, so use a different one if 1080 is already being used on your system.
   _NOTE:__the foo.sock and foo.state files do not need to exist. Only the directory (_~/.config/tailscale-work) _needs to exist. The tailscaled daemon will create and use the foo.sock and foo.state files for you._
